context:
Vibrations and waves are everywhere. If you take any system and disturb it from a stable equilibrium, the resultant motion will be waves and vibrations. Think of a guitar string—pluck the string, and it vibrates. The sound waves generated make their way to our ears, and we hear the string’s sound. Our eyes see what’s happening because they receive the electromagnetic waves of the light reflected from the guitar string, so that we can recognize the beautiful sinusoidal waves on the string. In fact, without vibrations and waves, we could not recognize the universe around us at all!

The amazing thing is that we can describe many fascinating phenomena arising from very different physical systems with mathematics. This course will provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. You will learn that waves come from many interconnected (coupled) objects when they are vibrating together. We will discuss many of these phenomena, along with related topics, including mechanical vibrations and waves, sound waves, electromagnetic waves, optics, and gravitational waves.

Syllabus:
8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves

Prerequisites:
18.02 Multivariable Calculus and 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism.

This course is designed for students who are already familiar with the concepts taught in 8.01 Classical Mechanics as well as 8.02 Electricity and Magnetism. We also rely on fundamental mathematics principles taught in 18.01 Single Variable Calculus and 18.02 Multivariable Calculus, as well as some aspects of 18.06 Linear Algebra.

Course Overview:
What are vibrations? Many physical systems vibrate or oscillate when something disrupts their equilibrium. Think of what happens when you pull a mass attached to a spring or push a pendulum in a certain direction. The mass bounces up and down. The pendulum swings back and forth. But even these simple phenomena can respond in complicated and counterintuitive ways when subjected to forces known in physics as damping and driving. Combining several pendulums or springs can lead to even more unexpected motions. In this course you will see how physics can describe these motions mathematically.

Extended physical systems that have been made to vibrate, like a string on a guitar, cannot return to their state of equilibrium without exerting forces on the area around them. These forces then lead to the phenomenon of waves, disturbances that propagate through a medium. The vibrating guitar string causes a sound wave to propagate through the medium of the air.

Among the most fascinating phenomena examined in the course are electromagnetic (EM) waves. EM waves have many properties similar to the waves we experience in everyday life, but the underlying physics of EM waves is profoundly different. Instead of a local disturbance exerting a force on adjoining regions, changing magnetic fields create electric fields (as quantified by Faraday's Law), and changing electric fields create magnetic fields (as quantified by Maxwell's extension of Ampere's Law).

Course Goals:
This course has two complementary goals. The first is to provide you with the concepts and mathematical tools necessary to understand and explain a broad range of vibrations and waves. This will allow you to gain a deeper appreciation for the true nature and beauty of phenomena like music and rainbows, which all of us observe or experience every day.

The second goal is to provide you with the skill of using a broad range of techniques that can greatly simplify the analysis and solution of complex systems. These techniques include complex numbers, combinations of oscillatory and exponentially decaying functions, resonance, normal modes, Cramer's rule for solving several equations in several variables, boundary conditions, general wave equations, Fourier decomposition, dispersive and non-dispersive media, phase and group velocities, sound cavities and wave guides, polarization, Doppler effect, reflection and refraction, Fresnel's equation for transmission and reflection, total internal reflection, constructive and destructive interference, and diffraction.

Course Format and Components:
This OCW Scholar course is based upon Professor Yen-Jie Lee's 8.03 Physics III: Vibrations and Waves course as taught on the MIT campus. It includes a complete set of 24 video lectures recorded at MIT in the Fall of 2016. Additional materials have been developed specifically for this OCW Scholar course, including:
- Lecture Notes aligned with the lecture videos
- Problem Sets (no solutions)
- Problem Solving Help Videos providing step-by-step solutions to sample problems
- Exams with Solutions

MIT students spend about 150-200 hours learning Vibrations and Waves in the on-campus version of this course. That number comes from a combination of attending lectures and recitations, and studying independently. It's difficult to estimate how long it will take you to complete all of the modules in this particular course, but you can probably expect to spend 3 to 4 hours on practice problems, readings and assessment for each hour of lecture video you watch.

Required Textbook:
Georgi, Howard. The Physics of Waves. Benjamin Cummings, 1992. ISBN: 9780136656210. Download the book (PDF - 5.5MB) (Courtesy of Howard Georgi. Used with permission.)

Suggested References:
French, A. P. Vibrations and Waves. New York: W.W. Norton, 1980. ISBN: 9780393099362.

Bekefi, George, and Alan H. Barrett. Electromagnetic Vibrations, Waves, and Radiation. MIT Press, 1977. ISBN: 9780262520478. [Preview with Google Books]
exercise:

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 4

8.03SC Physics III: Vibrations and Waves
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Fall 2016
Homework 4


Problem 4.1 (25 pts)

In the beaded string shown in Figure 1, the interval between neighboring beads is a, and the distance from the end beads to the wall is a/2. All the beads have mass m and are constrained to move only vertically in the plane of the paper. The strings are massless with constant string tension T.

[Figure 1: Beads on a string — beads numbered 1, 2, 3, ..., N−1, N spaced a apart, with the end beads a distance a/2 from each fixed wall.]

(a) Show that the physics of the left-hand wall can be incorporated by going to an infinite system and requiring the boundary condition A1 = −A0.
(b) Find the analogous boundary condition for the right-hand wall.
(c) Find the normal modes and the corresponding frequencies for the finite system.


Problem 4.2 (25 pts)

A physicist was trying to understand a system with two coupled torsional pendula. First, she tried to write down the equations of motion of the pendula in terms of θ1 and θ2, which are the angles with respect to the equilibrium positions of the pendula. She found that the interaction matrix M^(−1)K commutes with the 2 × 2 reflection symmetry matrix
    S = [ 0  −1 ]
        [ −1  0 ]
(i.e., satisfies the condition [S, M^(−1)K] = 0).

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) What will be the amplitude ratio (A1/A2, where A1 and A2 are the components of the eigenvector) of the torsional pendula in each normal mode?


Problem 4.3 (25 pts)

Consider a uniform thin string of length L and mass density µ. The string is attached at both ends to vertical, frictionless rods via massless rings as shown in Figure 2. The tension in the string is T.

[Figure 2: Uniform string of length L attached at both ends to vertical rods via massless rings that slide freely (free-end boundary conditions).]

(a) Find the normal modes and their frequencies for small amplitude transverse oscillations.
(b) Sketch the shapes of the first three normal modes.
(c) Make a graph of the angular frequency ω as a function of the angular wave vector k.


Problem 4.4 (25 pts)

A string of mass density µ and stretched with tension T is deformed as shown in Figure 3 (amplitude A is very small; the deformation as shown in the figure is greatly exaggerated). The string is released at t = 0 with zero initial velocity (∂y(x,t)/∂t = 0 at t = 0 for all x).

[Figure 3: String deformation — a triangular shape on the interval [−L, L], equal to 0 at x = −L, rising to +A at x = −L/2, passing through 0 at x = 0, dropping to −A at x = L/2, and returning to 0 at x = L.]

(a) How many normal modes do we have in this system?
(b) Find expressions for the Fourier coefficients Am of the normal mode expansion of the initial string shape and use them to write a full time-dependent series that describes the motion of the string at t > 0: y(x,t).
(c) Make a sketch of the amplitudes Am of the modes as a function of mode number m. Make sure that you indicate amplitudes for all possible normal modes of the string.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: I’m working on Problem Set 4 for vibrations and waves, but I’m kind of lost on all of it. Could we start with Problem 4.1 and maybe just tell me the big picture of what I’m supposed to do there?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, let's start with 4.1! Before I say anything, tell me: in the beaded string system, what does a fixed wall actually *do* to the displacement of the string at that point?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is just starting and asking for the big picture of 4.1. I should orient them without giving away any part of the solution. A good Socratic opening is to probe what they already know about the beaded string system and boundary conditions, rather than summarizing the problem for them. I'll ask a targeted question to activate prior knowledge and get them thinking in the right direction.

exchanges.student: I’m not totally sure, which is part of why I’m stuck. I think the wall means the displacement at the endpoint is zero, but then the problem uses these bead amplitudes and that \(A_1=-A_0\) thing, and I don’t see how those connect.
exchanges.tutor: Good — you're right that the wall forces zero displacement at that point. Now, in the extended infinite system, there's an imaginary bead at position 0 (just outside the wall). If the displacement at the wall must be zero, what mathematical condition would the amplitudes of the bead at position 0 and the bead at position 1 need to satisfy to make that happen?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified that the wall enforces zero displacement at the endpoint, but doesn't see how that connects to the A1=-A0 condition. This is good progress. I should affirm the correct insight and then guide them with a Socratic question toward the idea of an imaginary bead at position 0 in the extended infinite system, and how requiring zero displacement at the wall position connects to the amplitudes of neighboring beads.

exchanges.student: I guess they’d have to cancel at the wall, so maybe equal and opposite? So \(A_0=-A_1\), but I’m not confident why it’s written that way instead of just saying the endpoint is zero. Also that worked, so I’m still pretty fuzzy on the bigger pattern for the rest of 4.1.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — and that's precisely the point of part (a): instead of imposing a constraint directly, you extend the system and let the antisymmetry of the imaginary bead do the work for you. Now, can you apply the same logic to the right wall for part (b)? What's the position of the last real bead and the imaginary one beyond it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly arrived at A0 = -A1 (equivalent to A1 = -A0) through reasoning about cancellation. I should affirm this briefly and then push them to think about why the 'extended system' trick is useful rather than just setting the endpoint to zero directly — this will help them see the bigger pattern. Then I can nudge them toward part (b) by asking the same question about the right wall.

exchanges.student: I’m getting mixed up again. Is it something like \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\)? I feel like I’m guessing indices more than understanding it. That didn’t fully click, so maybe I should try a different angle: can I just get a broad overview of how parts (a), (b), and (c) fit together before worrying about the exact index bookkeeping?
exchanges.tutor: You're close on the index — just double-check the geometry: the right wall is also at distance a/2 from bead N, so the imaginary bead sits at position N+1. Does your condition still hold? As for the big picture: (a) and (b) give you the two boundary conditions, and (c) uses both together to find which wavelengths actually fit — that's where the normal modes come from. Try pinning down (b) first, then we'll move to (c).
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is expressing some frustration and asking for a broad overview. They're close on the index (though the geometry needs checking — the right end bead is at distance a/2 from the wall, same as the left). I should acknowledge they're on the right track with the index guess, give a small hint about the geometry to resolve the confusion, and briefly frame how a/b/c connect without giving away the solution. I won't give a full overview but I can give a one-sentence framing of the logical chain.

exchanges.student: Yeah, then I think it is \(A_{N+1}=-A_N\). I’m still not seeing how those boundary conditions actually turn into allowed wavelengths, though—could I see the general form written out, like the trial solution and what gets plugged into what?
exchanges.tutor: Good, that's the right boundary condition for (b)! But I won't write it out for you — that's your job. What does a normal mode solution for a beaded string look like? What's the general form you'd write for the amplitude of bead n?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student confirmed A_{N+1} = -A_N which is correct. Now they're asking me to write out the trial solution and the substitution process for them — that would be doing the work for them, which I should not do. Instead, I should ask them what they already know about the form of a normal mode solution for an infinite beaded string, activating prior knowledge and letting them do the writing.

exchanges.student: I think it’s something like \(A_n = C e^{ikna}\) or maybe a mix of sines and cosines, but I don’t know which version I’m supposed to use here. I’m honestly tempted to just ask for the full derivation because I can’t tell what choice is natural.
exchanges.tutor: I won't provide the derivation — that's yours to work through. But you've got the right options. Think about the boundary condition A0 = -A1: which of your two forms naturally gives zero at n=0 without extra work — the exponential, or the sine?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for the full derivation, which I must refuse. They've correctly identified both possible forms of the solution. I should affirm that both are valid and ask a targeted question to help them figure out which form is more natural given the boundary conditions they just derived — specifically, the antisymmetry condition A1=-A0 suggests a sine-like form. This is a good Socratic nudge without giving the answer.

exchanges.student: I guess sine feels more natural since it can encode zeros/antisymmetry more directly, but then I’m not sure what argument to use because the wall is at a half-spacing, not exactly at a bead. I’m still pretty lost, and that approach didn’t suddenly clear it up.
exchanges.tutor: You're thinking about it the right way. Don't worry about the wall's physical position for now — just try plugging your sine form A_n = sin(kna) into the condition A_0 = -A_1 and see what it tells you. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is on the right track with sine but is getting hung up on the half-spacing detail. This is a good specific confusion to address with a targeted question. I should ask them to just try plugging their sine form into the boundary condition A0 = -A1 and see what happens — rather than worrying abstractly about the half-spacing. This keeps them doing the work.

exchanges.student: If I do that, \(A_0=0\), so the condition says \(0=-\sin(ka)\), which would force \(\sin(ka)=0\). That seems way too restrictive, so I’m guessing plain \(\sin(kna)\) is the wrong form. Could I get a hint about the shifted argument without jumping all the way to the answer?
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning — you caught the problem yourself. Think about this: what if the argument accounts for the fact that the wall sits at a half-spacing? Try writing the sine with a shift of a/2 in the position, and check whether it satisfies A_0 = -A_1 automatically.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified that plain sin(kna) is too restrictive because it forces sin(ka)=0 immediately. This is good reasoning. They're asking for a hint about a shifted argument. I can give a small nudge: ask them what happens if they shift the argument by a/2, i.e., use sin(k(n-1/2)a) or similar — without writing it out fully. This keeps them doing the work while resolving the specific sticking point.

exchanges.student: Oh, so maybe I should use something like \(A_n \propto \sin(k(n-\tfrac12)a)\) or \(\sin(k(n+\tfrac12)a)\)? I’m not sure which sign matches the indexing, but that at least feels more sensible.
exchanges.tutor: Good instinct! Now just test both against your boundary condition A_0 = -A_1. Which one satisfies it?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at the right form on their own. I should affirm this and ask them to check both options against the boundary condition A0 = -A1 to determine which sign works — keeping them doing the work rather than just telling them the answer.

exchanges.student: I tested them and I think the half-shifted sine works, but I’m getting turned around on the sign and don’t trust my algebra. I think it’s the version that makes the wall sit at a node, but could you check which one actually satisfies \(A_0=-A_1\)?
exchanges.tutor: I won't check it for you, but show me your calculation for both cases — what did you get when you plugged n=0 and n=1 into each version? Then we can see together where the algebra is tripping you up.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to check their algebra for them, which would be doing the work for them. I should refuse this and instead ask them to show me what they computed for each case so I can point out where their reasoning goes right or wrong, rather than just confirming the answer.
